Category: Agriculture & Resources: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing, Mining, Energy
Description: Risks of relying on AI for ESG and environmental reporting.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with environmental or agricultural business operations, along with a general awareness of sustainability reporting concepts such as ESG frameworks, is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with using AI tools for ESG and environmental reporting within agriculture, forestry, fishing, mining, and energy sectors, and explain how inaccurate or biased AI outputs can undermine sustainability metrics. You will also apply practical frameworks to evaluate and verify AI-generated data, ensuring your organisation's environmental reporting remains credible, transparent, and aligned with recognised sustainability standards.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces learners to the growing use of artificial intelligence in sustainability reporting and explores why relying on AI tools without proper oversight can create significant risks for organisations. You will examine how AI systems can produce inaccurate, incomplete, or misleading environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data, and why this matters for regulatory compliance and stakeholder trust. The lesson covers practical ways to identify common AI-related errors in sustainability metrics, including issues around data sourcing, transparency, and accountability. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how to critically evaluate AI-generated sustainability information and apply appropriate checks before using it in formal reporting. No prior knowledge of AI or ESG frameworks is required, making this an accessible starting point for anyone working with or considering sustainability data in their organisation.
lessons.content: Risks of relying on AI for ESG and environmental reporting.

slides.subtitle: Where AI meets environmental accountability
slides.slidecontent:
• AI shapes ESG reporting now
• Errors spread fast, unseen
• Data gaps distort results
• Greenwashing risks increase
• Accountability becomes harder

slides.subtitle: Where AI Reporting Can Go Wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools can generate ESG figures that sound credible but lack verified data sources
• Outdated training data leading to inaccurate emissions benchmarks
• Greenwashing risks when unreviewed AI outputs are published directly
• No audit trail
• Inconsistent terminology across AI-generated reports causing compliance gaps

slides.subtitle: Flawed data corrupts AI-generated reports.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI outputs are only as reliable as the data fed into them
• Incomplete emissions records skew carbon footprint calculations
• Outdated supplier data leads to inaccurate scope 3 reporting
• Errors compound
• Mislabelled datasets can cause AI to misclassify environmental risks entirely
• Small input mistakes produce large, misleading ESG conclusions

slides.subtitle: Poor inputs corrupt ESG reporting outputs.
slides.slidecontent:
• Wrong data, wrong results
• Errors compound quickly
• Garbage in, garbage out
• Biased inputs skew scores
• AI cannot fix bad data

slides.subtitle: Where AI knowledge falls short
slides.slidecontent:
• AI training data often cuts off months or years before your reporting date
• No live feed of regulatory updates, new emissions factors, or revised GHG protocols
• Rapidly evolving standards (like TCFD or CSRD) may be partially or wholly absent
• Gaps you cannot see
• Regional and sector-specific data frequently underrepresented, skewing outputs
• Always verify AI-generated figures against current, authoritative sources before submission

slides.subtitle: Verifying What the Algorithms Produce
slides.slidecontent:
• Check sources behind figures
• Spot missing or vague data
• Compare outputs to originals
• Test for repeated errors
• Involve human reviewers always

slides.subtitle: Verification steps for trustworthy AI outputs
slides.slidecontent:
• Cross-check figures manually
• Use verified data sources
• Question unexpected results
• Document every AI output
• Review before publishing

slides.subtitle: Core principles for responsible AI reporting
slides.slidecontent:
• Always verify AI-generated ESG figures against primary data sources before reporting
• Hallucinated metrics can appear credible
• Understand the limitations of any AI tool used in your sustainability workflow
• Human oversight is non-negotiable when data informs regulatory or investor disclosures
• Document every step of your reporting process, including where AI was involved
• Start small by piloting AI tools on low-risk internal reports before using them in official submissions

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on Sustainability Metrics and AI Integrity. We're going to be exploring a topic that is becoming increasingly important for anyone working with ESG data and reporting. Artificial intelligence is now deeply embedded in how organisations collect, analyse, and communicate their sustainability performance. That might sound like progress, and in many ways it is, but it also introduces some serious risks that we need to understand. When AI systems make errors, those errors can ripple through reports and decisions very quickly, often without anyone noticing. If the underlying data has gaps or inconsistencies, the results can be misleading, even when everything looks polished on the surface. This creates real opportunities for greenwashing, where organisations appear more sustainable than they actually are. And when things go wrong, it becomes genuinely difficult to work out who is responsible. So throughout this course, we will look at how to recognise these risks and how to manage them responsibly.

So let's talk about where things can actually go wrong when AI gets involved in ESG reporting. One of the biggest issues is that AI tools can produce figures that sound completely plausible, but when you dig into them, there's no verified source behind the numbers. On top of that, many AI systems are trained on data that simply isn't current, which means the emissions benchmarks they reference could already be out of date. There's also a real greenwashing risk if organisations publish AI-generated content without reviewing it first. And here's something that often gets overlooked: there's frequently no audit trail, so if someone asks how a particular figure was calculated, you may have no way to answer that. Finally, AI tools don't always use consistent terminology, and in a compliance context, that inconsistency can create genuine gaps in your reporting. These are the risks we need to understand before we go any further in this course.

There's an old saying in computing: garbage in, garbage out. And it applies perfectly here. When we feed AI systems poor quality data, we shouldn't be surprised when the outputs are unreliable. Think about it this way. If your emissions records have gaps, your AI-generated carbon footprint report will be wrong from the start. If the information about your suppliers is out of date, your scope 3 reporting will be inaccurate. And here's the thing that catches a lot of people off guard: errors don't just sit there quietly. They compound. One small mistake early in the process can snowball into something much larger. If a dataset has been mislabelled, the AI might completely misclassify an environmental risk. A tiny input error can produce an ESG conclusion that is wildly misleading. So before we even think about what AI can do for sustainability reporting, we need to be honest about the quality of the data we're putting in.

There is an old saying in computing that has never lost its relevance: garbage in, garbage out. And when it comes to ESG reporting powered by AI, this principle matters enormously. If the data you feed into your systems is inaccurate, incomplete, or biased, the results you get back will be just as flawed, and sometimes worse. Here is the thing about errors in data: they do not stay small. They compound. One wrong figure in your supply chain emissions data can ripple through your entire sustainability score, distorting the picture in ways that are genuinely difficult to trace. And if your inputs carry bias, perhaps certain suppliers are consistently under-reported, then your AI model will simply bake that bias into every output it produces. The important point to understand here is that AI is not a correction tool. It cannot magically fix poor quality data. It amplifies whatever you give it. So the quality of your inputs is absolutely everything.

One of the most important things to understand about AI tools is that they are not connected to the internet in real time. Their knowledge comes from training data that was collected at some point in the past, which could be many months or even a couple of years before you are actually using the tool. That means any regulatory updates, revised emissions factors, or new reporting frameworks like TCFD or CSRD may simply not be in there at all, or only partially. And here is the tricky part: the AI will not always tell you when it does not know something. It will often produce a confident-looking answer even when its information is out of date. On top of that, data covering specific regions or industry sectors tends to be underrepresented, which can quietly skew your results. So the golden rule is this: always cross-check any figures or guidance the AI gives you against current, authoritative sources before you submit anything.

So, we've established that AI can generate ESG data quickly, but speed means nothing if the data isn't trustworthy. That's where auditing comes in. When you receive AI-generated figures, always trace them back to their original sources. Ask yourself: where did this number actually come from? You also want to watch out for gaps or vague language, because AI systems sometimes fill in missing information with plausible-sounding but inaccurate content. A really useful habit is to place the AI output side by side with the raw source material and compare them directly. You should also look for patterns of repeated errors, because AI tools can make the same mistake consistently, which makes it easy to miss if you're not paying attention. And perhaps most importantly, never rely on the algorithm alone. Human reviewers need to be part of this process at every stage. AI is a tool, not a replacement for professional judgement.

So, once you've got your AI-generated sustainability data, the real work begins: making sure you can actually trust it before it goes anywhere near a report or a presentation. The first thing to do is manually check your key figures. Don't just accept a number because the AI produced it. Cross-reference it against verified, reputable data sources to confirm it holds up. If something looks surprising or doesn't quite fit the pattern you'd expect, treat that as a signal to investigate rather than ignore it. Unexpected results aren't always wrong, but they always deserve a closer look. It's also really important to keep a clear record of every output the AI generates, including the prompts you used and when you ran them. That audit trail becomes invaluable if anyone questions your figures later. And finally, always build in a proper review stage before anything gets published. A fresh pair of eyes can catch what you might have missed.

So let's bring together the core principles we've covered in this lesson. The most important habit you can build is always checking AI-generated figures against your original data sources, because AI can produce numbers that look completely convincing but are simply wrong. That's not a flaw you can spot just by reading the output carefully, which is exactly why human oversight is essential, especially when your data is going into regulatory filings or investor reports. You also need to understand the specific limitations of whatever AI tool you're using in your workflow, and document every step of your process, including where and how AI was involved. And if you're just getting started, please don't pilot a new AI tool on a high risk official submission. Begin with something low-risk, like an internal report, so you can build confidence before the stakes are higher. These principles aren't complicated, but they do require consistency and discipline every single time.
